author: Sofia Martínez
title: AI Email Personalization Has Moved Way Beyond Merge Tags
description: First-name merge tags were the first generation. The teams outperforming benchmarks are doing something structurally different with AI.

sections.p: Merge tags were the first generation of email personalization. Subject line with first name, company name in the opening line, maybe industry-specific language if the copywriter was diligent. Every team that benchmarks email now knows that generation is table stakes. The teams pulling 40%+ open rates and double-digit click rates have moved to something structurally different.

The shift is from who the recipient is to what they've done. A first-name merge tag uses a static field. An AI system that personalizes on last-clicked content, time since last purchase, feature usage, and support ticket history is doing something no human copywriter could scale to.

Klaviyo's AI, Braze, and a growing list of challengers now let you define personalization rules in natural language and have the system write distinct email variants for each behavioral cohort. You write one brief. The AI ships 40 variants.

sections.h2: The architecture: segment → signal → brief → variant

list: Define 5–10 behavioral micro-segments based on product data, not demographics.
Map 2–3 high-signal data points per segment (e.g., last feature used, days since onboarding, plan tier).
Write a master brief that captures offer, tone, and CTA.
Let the AI generate and A/B test variants per segment automatically.
Review output for brand voice before sending — AI writes the draft, humans approve.

sections.h2: Subject line AI is table stakes now

sections.p: Every major ESP has native subject line AI. At this point, not using it is leaving open rate points on the table for free. What's still differentiated is using AI to rewrite body copy length by segment — power users get a dense technical version, trial users get a lighter benefit-led version of the same email.

AI personalization that pulls in behavioral data needs to be GDPR and CAN-SPAM compliant by design. That means consent-based data only, clear opt-down paths for each content type, and no inference that would surprise a recipient if they knew it was happening.
The teams that skip this step eventually get to a high-performing email program that they have to tear down and rebuild. Build it compliant from the start.

sections.quote: The best AI email programs don't feel personalized. They feel relevant. The difference is whether you're using demographics or behavior.
